section.title: Saul Meets Samuel
section.sectionRationale: God begins to answer Israel's demand. A tall, handsome young Benjamite named Saul goes searching for his father's lost donkeys and, guided by a servant and by God's hidden hand, comes to the town where Samuel is. The day before, God had told Samuel He would send the man to anoint as ruler. Samuel welcomes Saul, honors him at the feast, and prepares to reveal God's word. It is one movement: the quiet, providential meeting of the prophet and the man Israel's demand has drawn onto the stage.
section.thesis: Through an ordinary errand after lost donkeys, God sovereignly brings Saul to Samuel, showing that the king Israel demanded comes only as God gives him, chosen and set apart by the LORD, so that even in granting a flawed request God remains the one who raises up rulers and directs every step.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Saul introduced and the lost donkeys, verses 1 to 5; the servant's counsel to seek the seer, verses 6 to 14; God's word to Samuel about the coming man, verses 15 to 17; and Samuel welcomes and honors Saul, verses 18 to 27.

units.label: Saul introduced and the lost donkeys

verses.text: Now there was a Benjamite, a powerful man, whose name was Kish son of Abiel, the son of Zeror, the son of Becorath, the son of Aphiah of Benjamin.
And he had a son named Saul, choice and handsome, without equal among the Israelites, a head taller than any of the people.
One day the donkeys of Saul’s father Kish wandered off, and Kish said to his son Saul, “Take one of the servants and go look for the donkeys.”

So Saul passed through the hill country of Ephraim and then through the land of Shalishah, but they did not find the donkeys. He and the servant went through the region of Shaalim, but they were not there. Then they went through the land of Benjamin, and still they did not find them.
When they reached the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant, “Come, let us go back, or my father will stop worrying about the donkeys and start worrying about us.”

units.label: The servant's counsel to seek the seer
units.note: there is a man of God in this city

verses.text: “Look,” said the servant, “in this city there is a man of God who is highly respected; everything he says surely comes to pass. Let us go there now. Perhaps he will tell us which way to go.”
“If we do go,” Saul replied, “what can we give the man? For the bread in our packs is gone, and there is no gift to take to the man of God. What do we have?”
The servant answered him again. “Look,” he said, “I have here in my hand a quarter shekel of silver. I will give it to the man of God, and he will tell us our way.”
(Formerly in Israel, a man on his way to inquire of God would say, “Come, let us go to the {{roeh|seer}}.” For the prophet of today was formerly called the seer.)
“Good,” said Saul to his servant. “Come, let us go.” So they set out for the city where the man of God was.
And as they were climbing the hill to the city, they met some young women coming out to draw water and asked, “Is the seer here?”
“Yes, he is ahead of you,” they answered. “Hurry now, for today he has come to the city because the people have a sacrifice on the high place.

As soon as you enter the city, you will find him before he goes up to the high place to eat. The people will not eat until he comes, because he must bless the sacrifice; after that, the guests will eat. Go up at once; you will find him.”
So Saul and his servant went up toward the city, and as they were entering it, there was Samuel coming toward them on his way up to the high place.

units.label: God's word to Samuel about the coming man
units.note: anoint him ruler over My people Israel

verses.text: Now on the day before Saul’s arrival, the LORD had revealed to Samuel,

“At this time tomorrow I will send you a man from the land of Benjamin, and you are to {{mashach|anoint}} him {{nagid|ruler}} over My people Israel; he will save them from the hand of the Philistines. For I have looked upon My people, because their cry has come to Me.”
When Samuel saw Saul, the LORD told him, “Here is the man of whom I spoke; he shall rule over My people.”

units.label: Samuel welcomes and honors Saul
units.note: seated in the place of honor

verses.text: Saul approached Samuel in the gateway and asked, “Would you please tell me where the seer’s house is?”
“I am the seer,” Samuel replied. “Go up before me to the high place, for you shall eat with me today. And when I send you off in the morning, I will tell you all that is in your heart.
As for the donkeys you lost three days ago, do not worry about them, for they have been found. And upon whom is all the desire of Israel, if not upon you and all your father’s house?”
Saul replied, “Am I not a Benjamite from the smallest tribe of Israel, and is not my clan the least of all the clans of Benjamin? So why would you say such a thing to me?”
Then Samuel took Saul and his servant, brought them into the hall, and seated them in the place of honor among those who were invited, about thirty in all.
And Samuel said to the cook, “Bring the portion I gave you and told you to set aside.”

So the cook picked up the leg and what was attached to it and set it before Saul. Then Samuel said, “Here is what was kept back. It was set apart for you. Eat, for it has been kept for you for this occasion, from the time I said, ‘I have invited the people.’” So Saul dined with Samuel that day.
And after they had come down from the high place into the city, Samuel spoke with Saul on the roof of his house.

They got up early in the morning, and just before dawn Samuel called to Saul on the roof, “Get ready, and I will send you on your way!” So Saul got ready, and both he and Samuel went outside together.

As they were going down to the edge of the city, Samuel said to Saul, “Tell the servant to go on ahead of us, but you stay for a while, and I will reveal to you the word of God.” So the servant went on.

roeh.gloss: Seer, one who sees. An older word for a prophet, one to whom God shows what others cannot see. The narrator explains that the seer of former days is the prophet of now. The irony runs deep in this chapter about a king chosen for how he looks: the true sight belongs to the seer, and finally to the LORD, who sees the heart.

mashach.gloss: To anoint, smear with oil. Anointing set a person apart to God for an office, especially king or priest, marking him as the LORD's chosen and equipped by His Spirit. From this verb comes mashiach, messiah. God tells Samuel to anoint Saul; the office is holy even when the man will fail, and it points ahead to the true Anointed One.

nagid.gloss: Leader, ruler, prince, one appointed over God's people. God calls Saul not first melek, king, but nagid, the one designated to rule under the LORD, over My people Israel. The word stresses that the ruler belongs to God and serves God's people; the king is never autonomous but always the LORD's appointed steward.

basic.context: God now begins to give Israel the king they demanded, but in a strikingly ordinary way. A tall, good-looking young man named Saul is out searching for his father's runaway donkeys. After days of fruitless walking, his servant suggests they ask the local man of God, Samuel, for guidance. Unknown to Saul, God had told Samuel the very day before that He would send him the man to anoint as ruler over Israel. When Saul appears, God says, this is the man. Samuel welcomes him, tells him the donkeys are found, hints that Saul is the one all Israel desires, and honors him at the feast. The question is: where does a king come from? Not from Israel's grasping demand alone, but from God's hidden, sovereign hand, working even through lost donkeys.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative full of quiet providence. God is never named as intervening in the donkey search, yet His hand is everywhere, steering Saul step by step to Samuel. We should notice how the chapter stresses Saul's impressive appearance, tall and handsome, because the story will later show that God does not choose as man chooses. It is not teaching that lost animals always lead to destiny; it shows that God sovereignly directs events to accomplish His purpose, even in granting a flawed request.
basic.originalLanguages: The narrator pauses to explain that the prophet was once called the roeh, the seer, the one who sees what others cannot, a pointed word in a chapter about a king chosen for his looks. God tells Samuel to mashach, to anoint, this man, setting him apart with oil as the LORD's chosen; from this verb comes the word messiah. And Saul is to be nagid, ruler, the one designated to lead over My people Israel, a leader who belongs to God and serves under Him.
basic.typology: The anointing of a ruler to save God's people from their enemies points forward to the true Anointed One, the Messiah, who saves His people from a greater enemy. Saul, tall and impressive yet destined to fail, stands as the foil to the King God really wants, chosen not for appearance but after God's own heart, and ultimately to Christ. The providence that guides an unwitting man to his calling reflects the God who works all things according to His purpose.
basic.christ: God says of Saul, anoint him ruler; he will save them from the Philistines. Saul is a first, faltering answer to Israel's need, but the word anointed, mashiach, reaches beyond him. Saul saves from the Philistines for a time; Christ, the true Anointed, saves His people from sin and death forever. Saul is chosen for his height and looks; Christ, having no outward majesty that we should desire Him, is the King God chooses to save. The office of anointed ruler, holy even in Saul's hands, finds its perfection in Jesus.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the smallest details, guiding lost donkeys, a servant's suggestion, and a young man's route to bring about His purpose. He is the God who hears, for He says their cry has come to Me, and He acts to save even a people who have rejected Him. He reveals His plans to His prophet ahead of time, showing He is never caught off guard. And He is the one who gives rulers; the king comes as God's gift and appointment, not merely as Israel's demand.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16 says the lot is cast but every decision is from the LORD. Romans 8 assures that God works all things together for good. 1 Samuel 16 will show God choosing by the heart, not appearance. And Acts 13 recounts God giving Saul and then raising up David and, from his line, Jesus the Savior.
basic.oneStory: God begins to grant Israel's demand by sovereignly leading Saul to Samuel to be anointed ruler. Yet the stress on Saul's looks and the word anointed already point beyond this tall Benjamite to the King after God's heart and to Christ, the true Anointed One who saves His people forever. Even in giving a flawed king, God is quietly steering the story toward the true King.

context.literary: This chapter begins the account of Saul's rise, chapters 9 to 11, following the demand for a king in chapter 8. It is rich in irony and providence: an errand for donkeys becomes the path to a throne. The emphasis on Saul's height and handsome appearance sets up the later contrast with David, whom God chooses by the heart, chapter 16.
context.historical: A seer or man of God was consulted for guidance, and bringing a small gift was customary. High places were local worship sites where sacrifices and communal meals were held. The place of honor and the reserved portion, the leg, marked Saul out as an honored guest. Benjamin was indeed a small tribe, nearly destroyed in Judges 20, which makes Saul's protest of unworthiness historically pointed.
context.canonical: The anointing of a nagid to save God's people begins the institution of kingship that runs through Samuel and Kings. The word anointed, mashiach, sounds again after Hannah's song and the oracle to Eli, building the messianic thread. Saul's selection by appearance, contrasted with David and finally Christ, develops the biblical theme that God sees not as man sees.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's king comes as God's sovereign gift and appointment, guided by His hidden providence, and to introduce Saul, impressive in appearance, as the one God will have Samuel anoint, even as the narrative quietly prepares us to look for a better king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's providential leading of Saul; it does not promise that ordinary errands will reveal one's destiny nor prescribe consulting seers. What endures is the truth of God's meticulous sovereignty, His hearing of His people's cry, and His role as the giver of rulers.

debates.question: How should we read the heavy emphasis on Saul's impressive appearance?

views.label: A subtle warning that Israel chose by outward standards
views.case: Saul is exactly the kind of king the nations would admire, tall and handsome; the narrator highlights this to expose that Israel wanted a king like the nations, by outward impressiveness, setting up the lesson of chapter 16 that God sees the heart.

views.label: A genuine sign of God's gracious gift
views.case: Saul's stature is a real endowment; God gives Israel an outwardly capable leader, and the appearance is not itself condemned but shows that God graciously grants a fitting man, whose later failure will lie in his heart, not his gifts.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter prepares the reader to value the heart over appearance, pointing forward to David, chosen by the heart, and to Christ, the King without outward majesty who truly saves.

debates.question: Is God's involvement here approval of the monarchy, given chapter 8 called it rejection?

views.label: Gracious accommodation, not reversal of the verdict
views.case: God still granted a request He called a rejection of Himself, yet He graciously oversees it, hearing His people's cry and providing a ruler to save them from the Philistines; His providence does not erase the earlier warning but shows mercy within judgment.

views.label: Sovereign turning of the monarchy toward His purpose
views.case: By personally choosing and anointing the ruler, God asserts that even the king Israel demanded is His to give and govern; He is bending the institution toward the eventual raising up of David and the Messiah.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: God can grant a flawed request as discipline and mercy at once, remaining sovereign over the outcome and steering it toward the true King who saves His people.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is quietly loaded with meaning. The narrator breaks in to explain an old term: the prophet used to be called the roeh, the seer, the one to whom God shows what others cannot see. It is a pointed word in a story about a king chosen for how he looks, for true sight belongs not to the tall man but to the seer, and finally to the LORD who sees the heart. God tells Samuel to mashach, to anoint, this man, to set him apart with oil as the LORD's own chosen and equipped one; from this very verb comes mashiach, messiah, so the office is holy even though Saul will fail. And Saul is to be nagid, ruler, before he is ever called melek, king: the nagid is the one designated to lead over My people Israel, a ruler who belongs to God and serves under Him, never autonomous. The words themselves hint that the true King is not this handsome Benjamite but the Anointed One yet to come.

typology.type: The anointed ruler who saves God's people from their enemies
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Anointed One, who saves His people from sin and death, Luke 4:18 and Matthew 1:21.

typology.type: The tall, impressive king chosen by outward standards
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who had no outward majesty that we should desire Him yet is the King God chooses to save, Isaiah 53:2.

typology.type: God's hidden providence leading a man to his calling
typology.fulfillment: The God who works all things according to the counsel of His will, bringing His purposes to pass in Christ, Ephesians 1:11.

study.christ: God tells Samuel, anoint him ruler over My people Israel; he will save them from the hand of the Philistines. Saul is the first, faltering answer to Israel's cry, but the word at the heart of the command, mashach, to anoint, reaches far beyond him. Saul will save from the Philistines for a season; Christ, the true Anointed, the Messiah to whom the word points, saves His people from sin and death forever. Saul is chosen because he is tall and handsome, a head above the rest; Christ, of whom Isaiah says He had no form or majesty that we should look at Him, is the King God chooses to accomplish salvation. And the office itself, anointed ruler over God's people, holy even when Saul dishonors it, finds its perfection in Jesus, who rules over God's people not by taking but by saving, and whose reign has no end. The chapter's quiet providence, steering a man through lost donkeys to his anointing, is the same sovereign hand that brought forth the Christ in the fullness of time.
study.god: This chapter reveals a God of exhaustive, gracious sovereignty. He governs the smallest and most ordinary things, wandering donkeys, a servant's passing suggestion, the exact route of a young man's search, to bring Saul to Samuel at the appointed hour. He is the God who hears, saying their cry has come to Me, and who moves to save even a people who have just rejected His kingship, showing mercy within His discipline. He reveals His plans to His prophet a full day ahead, proving He is never surprised or reactive but always purposeful. And He is the true giver of rulers; the king Israel demanded still comes only as God chooses, anoints, and sends him. Such a God can be trusted to weave even our flawed choices into His good and saving purpose.

crossReferences.note: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, the hidden providence that guides Saul to Samuel.
The LORD looks not at outward appearance but at the heart, the lesson set up by the stress on Saul's height.
The true King had no outward majesty that we should desire Him, unlike the tall and handsome Saul.
God works all things according to the counsel of His will, the sovereignty at work through the donkey search.

study.oneStory: God begins to grant Israel's demand by leading Saul, through an errand for lost donkeys, straight to Samuel to be anointed ruler over His people. Yet the narrator's stress on Saul's impressive looks and the weight of the word anointed already point past this tall Benjamite to the King chosen after God's own heart and to Christ, the true Anointed One who saves His people forever. Even in giving a flawed king, the sovereign God is steering the whole story toward the true King who will not fail.

quiz.q: How did Saul come to meet Samuel?

options.text: While searching for his father's lost donkeys, guided by his servant and by God's hidden providence, he came to Samuel's town.
options.note: Verses 3 to 14 show an ordinary errand steered by God to bring Saul to the prophet.

options.text: He went looking for the man of God to ask about the lost donkeys.
options.note: True at the human level, but it omits God's sovereign hand directing the whole search.

options.text: Samuel summoned Saul to the palace to crown him.
options.note: There was no palace; they met providentially as Saul sought lost donkeys.

options.text: Saul led an army to Samuel to demand the throne.
options.note: Saul came humbly searching for donkeys, not leading an army.

quiz.q: What had God revealed to Samuel the day before Saul arrived?

options.text: That He would send a man from Benjamin to be anointed ruler, who would save Israel from the Philistines.
options.note: Verses 15 to 16; God announces His choice in advance, showing His sovereign purpose.

options.text: That a tall young man would come looking for lost donkeys.
options.note: God did send that man, but the revelation was specifically about anointing a ruler to save Israel.

options.text: That Israel would soon repent of asking for a king.
options.note: God spoke of providing the ruler, not of Israel repenting of the request.

options.text: That Samuel should refuse to anoint anyone.
options.note: God told Samuel to anoint the man, not refuse.

quiz.q: Why does the narrator stress that Saul was tall and handsome?

options.text: To highlight that Israel wanted a king by outward standards, setting up the later lesson that God sees the heart.
options.note: The emphasis prepares for chapter 16, where God chooses David by the heart, not appearance.

options.text: To show that God gave Israel an outwardly capable leader.
options.note: A fair point, but the deeper purpose is to contrast outward looks with the heart God values.

options.text: Simply to record what Saul looked like, with no larger point.
options.note: The detail is deliberate, pointing to the theme of appearance versus the heart.

options.text: To prove that God always chooses the most impressive person.
options.note: The story leads to the opposite: God looks at the heart, not appearance.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Saul was to be anointed nagid, ruler over God's people?

options.text: He was set apart by God to lead under the LORD, belonging to God and serving His people, not ruling autonomously.
options.note: Nagid stresses the ruler is God's appointed steward over My people Israel.

options.text: He was chosen by God to be the leader of Israel.
options.note: True, but it misses that nagid stresses ruling under God, not on his own authority.

options.text: He was given absolute power to do as he pleased.
options.note: The opposite; the nagid rules under God, accountable to Him.

options.text: He was appointed only as a temporary military captain.
options.note: He was anointed ruler over God's people, more than a passing captain.

quiz.q: How does the anointing of Saul point forward to Christ?

options.text: The word anointed, mashiach, and the calling to save God's people point beyond Saul to Christ, the true Anointed One who saves forever.
options.note: Saul saves from the Philistines for a time; Christ, the Messiah, saves from sin and death.

options.text: It shows that God provides leaders to rescue His people.
options.note: True, but it stops short of the Messiah, the true Anointed, whom the word foreshadows.

options.text: It teaches that the tallest person should always lead.
options.note: The story moves the other way, toward the King chosen by the heart, not height.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The anointing and the title Messiah clearly point to Jesus.

questions: God guided Saul to Samuel through something as ordinary as lost donkeys. Where might God be at work in the ordinary interruptions and detours of your life?
Israel wanted a king who looked the part. Where are you tempted to judge yourself or others by outward appearance rather than by the heart God sees?
God said their cry has come to Me, and He acted to save even a people who had rejected Him. How does God's mercy within His discipline encourage you when you have failed?
Saul protested that he was from the smallest tribe and least clan. How can a humble sense of your own smallness be the right place to receive God's calling?
The word anointed points past Saul to Christ, the true Anointed One. How does knowing Jesus as your King and Savior reshape the way you seek security and leadership?

translationNotes.note: The source has an em dash before a head taller; rendered here as a comma. Saul's impressive stature is stressed to set up the later contrast with David.
The narrator's aside explains that the older term seer, roeh, is the same office as the later prophet, navi, a note added for readers of a later time.
Anoint him ruler renders mashach and nagid; God designates Saul as leader under Himself, and the verb anoint is the root of the word messiah.
The source has an em dash before about thirty in all; rendered here as a comma. The place of honor and reserved portion mark Saul as the honored guest.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 9:1-27. Two em dashes, in verses 2 and 22, were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
